nativeRange.habitat: Rocky and coral reefs, typically at 3–70 m depth; juveniles often shelter in branching corals while adults patrol open reef structure and forage over sand and rubble.

diet.notes: Feeds on a variety of meaty foods. In the wild, adults are documented eating brittle stars, crustaceans, molluscs, and sea urchins, while juveniles act as cleaners removing parasites from larger fish. In captivity, generally accepts mysis, frozen krill, chopped shellfish, squid, and other meaty marine preparations. Tends to forage actively over rock and substrate.

summary: The Spanish Hogfish is a colorful Western Atlantic reef fish with a distinctive purple-blue upper body and bright yellow underside. Juveniles are known to act as cleaner fish, picking parasites from other species; adults tend to become semi-aggressive and will prey on small invertebrates.
careNotes:
The Spanish Hogfish is generally considered an intermediate-level marine fish. Juveniles are typically peaceful and have been observed removing parasites from tankmates, but as they mature they tend to become semi-aggressive and have been known to bully smaller, more docile species. They are commonly considered incompatible with reef invertebrates — snails, ornamental shrimp, small crabs, worms, clams, and sea urchins are all at risk — so this species is generally best housed in a fish-only or fish-only-with-live-rock (FOWLR) system. A FOWLR tank uses live rock for biological filtration and habitat structure but does not include corals or invertebrate livestock.

The Spanish Hogfish is an active swimmer that benefits from ample open water column space alongside rocky hiding places and caves. It is generally regarded as hardy and disease-resistant for a reef fish.

In the wild, the Spanish Hogfish is a protogynous hermaphrodite — meaning individuals are born female and some later transition to male — and forms harem groups of one dominant male with several smaller females, with the sex reversal completing in approximately 7–10 days.
breedingNotes: The Spanish Hogfish has been reported to spawn in pairs at dusk, releasing eggs that drift in the water column (known as pelagic eggs) until they hatch. As a protogynous hermaphrodite — a type of sequential sex change where individuals begin life as female — some individuals later transition to male, particularly when a dominant male is absent. In the wild this species forms harem groups with one male and several females. Reliable breeding in a home aquarium has not been documented.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Product page with care sheet. Listed minimum tank 150 gal, temperature 72–78°F, SG 1.020–1.025, pH 8.1–8.4, max size 10 in, care level easy, temperament aggressive toward smaller fish.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Labridae, Eupercaria), max size (40 cm TL, common 28 cm), native range (Western Atlantic, Bermuda/Florida to southern Brazil), depth (1–70 m), wild temperature 22–28°C, and confirmed protogynous hermaphrodite with harem grouping. FishBase temperature is somewhat warmer than LiveAquaria's 72–78°F; averaged across sources.

additional.name: ReefApp
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (40 cm), reef safety ('with caution' / invertebrate hunter), and tank volume (1500 L). Tank size is substantially larger than LiveAquaria's 150 gal / 568 L; the >2× spread triggered needs_review.

additional.name: FreshMarine
additional.notes: Additional cross-check: minimum tank 155 gal for adults, temperature 72–78°F, SG 1.020–1.025, pH 8.1–8.4, max size up to 16 in, care level moderate, semi-aggressive, reef safe with caution.

fish.breedingNotes: Captive breeding has not been reliably documented. Oviparous with pelagic eggs; protogynous hermaphrodite that forms harems in the wild.
